preFix.title: Italic proportions — extended and compressed
preFix.subtitle: Two width settings built from the same underlying structure
preFix.excerpt: Standard Italic has an o width of roughly five nib-widths across the body. Extend the o to six or seven nib-widths and every letter broadens with it, producing a hand suited to display headings. Compress the o to three or four nib-widths and the whole alphabet narrows, producing a hand suited to text blocks where horizontal space is limited. Both variations use the same nib angle and slope as standard Italic; only the width model of the o changes.
preFix.sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906): public domain, Internet Archive. Johnston's analysis of the Italic hand provides the structural framework for proportional variations. Alfred Fairbank, A Handwriting Manual (1932) is still in copyright (Fairbank died 1982; life + 70 applies in the UK); this entry draws on structural description of the o model only, not from Fairbank's text directly.

content.text: The same Italic hand can serve as a light, open display script, a standard running hand, or a tight text-setting hand. The mechanism is the

: the width of the o drives the width of every other letter in the alphabet, because all letter bodies are proportioned relative to the o. Change the o and the whole hand changes with it. The nib angle (45 degrees), the
(5 to 8 degrees), and the x-height (5 nib-widths) remain constant; only the lateral stretch of the o changes.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, 3 (medium)
items.substitutions: Any broad-edge nib of a consistent width; the Brause Bandzug is slightly stiffer and suits compressed work

items.name: Brause Bandzug nib (optional)
items.substitutions: Alternative to the Mitchell for compressed-hand practice; the firmer nib holds the tight strokes well

content.text: Rule guide lines for all three widths on three separate sheets before beginning. Use the nib-width stacking method: place the nib sideways on the paper and mark each stack for the x-height. For standard Italic, the x-height is 5 nib-widths; the ascenders and descenders extend a further 3 to 4 nib-widths above and below. All three sheets share the same x-height. The width difference appears in the o, not in the vertical spacing.

Establish the standard o first, on the middle sheet. Hold the nib at 45 degrees. Draw the o as two strokes: a left arc from the top of the letter down and round to the baseline, and a right arc closing the form. The standard o body should be roughly as wide as the x-height: about 5 nib-widths. Make ten os, then measure the average width. This is your standard o reference.
Move to the extended-width sheet. Draw the o wider: the two arcs open further to the sides, producing a body that is 6 to 7 nib-widths across. The

stays the same; only the lateral spread changes. The extended o looks nearly circular rather than elliptical. From this extended o, write the full alphabet, carrying the same width through every letter: the n arches are wider, the a counter is wider, the bowl of the p is wider.

Move to the compressed-width sheet. Draw the o narrow: the two arcs close toward the vertical, producing a body that is 3 to 4 nib-widths across. The compressed o looks like a narrow vertical ellipse. Carry this width through the alphabet: every letter body narrows accordingly. The n is almost double-minim narrow; the a counter is a tight space; the letter g has a very small lower bowl.

to match the width. Extended letters need slightly wider spaces between them: the counter of the extended o sets the target interspace. Compressed letters need slightly less interspace than standard: if the standard interspace is used with a compressed alphabet, the words look gappy. Write the word 'minimum' in each width setting and compare: the spatial rhythm of the page should feel even across all three settings.

The slope remains constant at 5 to 8 degrees in all three variants. Do not compensate for the change in width by altering the slope. Extended Italic with a shallower slope reads as Roman rather than Italic; compressed Italic with a steeper slope reads as blackletter. Hold the slope constant and let only the width change.

Where to use each width: extended Italic works well for headings, short display lines, and subheadings where the horizontal space is not constrained. Standard Italic suits body text and quotations. Compressed Italic suits text in columns or narrow panels, verse layouts where line length must be shortened, and situations where a lot of text must fit a small space without reducing the nib size.

attrs.intro: Most difficulties with width variants come from inconsistency. The o width needs to carry through the full alphabet. The common error is drifting back to the standard o width part-way through a word.

items.fix: Write only the letter o and the letter n for one full practice row in the extended proportion before attempting any other letters. The n is the single most reliable width-calibration letter in the Italic alphabet.
items.cause: The o model was set correctly. But on familiar letters, standard Italic muscle memory takes over.
items.symptom: The extended alphabet drifts back toward standard width after a few letters

items.fix: Check the slope with a protractor or angle guide: it should be 5 to 8 degrees. The nib angle should still be 45 degrees. Compressed Italic maintains a light, forward lean; blackletter is vertical.
items.cause: The slope has been increased to match the compressed width, or the nib angle has been made steeper.
items.symptom: The compressed alphabet looks like blackletter

items.fix: Write the word 'on' in the extended proportion and study the space between the o and the n. Reduce the space between all letter pairs to match the counter of the extended o. The word should look as rhythmically even as a standard-Italic word.
items.cause: Standard interspace is being used with extended letters.
items.symptom: Words in extended Italic look too widely spaced

attrs.heading: Common problems

content.text: Width variation gives calligraphy work a full range of typographic expression without changing the script or the tool. A piece using extended Italic for the heading, standard for the body, and compressed for a footnote has three distinct visual weights. All three are written with the same hand and the same nib. The next step is applying the same width logic to the Foundational hand, which follows the same proportion rules.

initialFindings.message: brand-trademark: brand name "Target" — generic equivalent is "(any general-purpose shop)"
title contains em or en dash — must be zero anywhere in user-facing copy
